meta.name: PCS Pain Catastrophizing Scale
meta.description: The PCS is a 13-item self-report scale that assesses catastrophic thinking related to pain experiences. It includes three dimensions: Helplessness, Magnification, and Rumination, and is widely used in pain management and clinical research.
meta.author: Sullivan, Bishop & Pivik (1995)
meta.reference: Sullivan MJL, Bishop SR, Pivik J. The Pain Catastrophizing Scale: development and validation. Psychol Assess. 1995;7(4):524-532.
meta.instruction: When you are in pain, you may have various thoughts and feelings. Please rate how often you experience each of these when in pain.

questions.text: I worry all the time about whether the pain will end
I feel I can’t go on
It’s terrible and I think it’s never going to get any better
It’s awful and I feel that it overwhelms me
I feel I can’t stand it anymore
I become afraid that the pain will get worse
I keep thinking of other painful events
I anxiously want the pain to go away
I can’t seem to keep it out of my mind
I keep thinking about how much it hurts
I keep thinking about how badly I want the pain to stop
There’s nothing I can do to reduce the intensity of the pain
I wonder whether something serious may happen

ranges.label: Normal
ranges.description: Your pain catastrophizing score is low, indicating that you generally cope well with pain experiences without excessive worry, rumination, or feelings of helplessness. You are able to maintain perspective about pain and its likely outcomes.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your effective and balanced approach to coping with pain experiences when they arise
Seek appropriate medical care for pain management when needed, while maintaining confidence in your ability to handle discomfort
Your healthy pain coping style is a protective factor; staying physically active and engaged in life helps sustain this resilience

ranges.label: Mild Catastrophizing
ranges.description: You show some tendency toward pain catastrophizing, occasionally exaggerating the threat of pain or feeling unable to cope. While not severe, these patterns can amplify pain perception and may interfere with effective pain management strategies.
suggestions: Learn evidence-based pain management skills such as pacing activities, relaxation techniques, and cognitive coping strategies
Practice mindfulness meditation, which has strong research support for reducing pain catastrophizing and improving pain tolerance
Maintain regular moderate physical activity, as movement is one of the most effective strategies for managing pain and preventing deconditioning
If pain is persistent or worsening, consult a pain management specialist who can offer a comprehensive multimodal treatment approach

ranges.label: Moderate Catastrophizing
ranges.description: Your pain catastrophizing is moderately elevated, suggesting that you frequently ruminate about pain, magnify its threat, or feel helpless when experiencing it. This pattern is clinically significant and associated with increased chronic pain risk and slower recovery.
suggestions: Consulting a pain psychologist or mental health professional with expertise in chronic pain management is recommended at this level
Cognitive-behavioral therapy for pain has strong evidence for reducing catastrophizing, improving coping, and decreasing pain-related disability
Practice separating the physical sensation of pain from your thoughts and emotional reactions about it, as these are distinct processes
Discuss a comprehensive pain management plan with your healthcare team that addresses both physical and psychological components of pain
Gradually increase engagement in valued activities despite pain, as avoidance often worsens both catastrophizing and functional disability

ranges.label: Severe Catastrophizing
ranges.description: Your pain catastrophizing score is very high, indicating intense and persistent rumination, magnification, and helplessness related to pain. This level significantly amplifies pain perception and is strongly associated with disability, depression, and poor treatment outcomes.
suggestions: Seeking help from a pain psychologist or multidisciplinary pain clinic is strongly recommended, as catastrophizing at this level requires professional intervention
A comprehensive treatment approach combining psychological therapy, medical management, and physical rehabilitation is likely to be most effective
Avoid the cycle of excessive pain monitoring and rumination by scheduling structured activities and focusing attention on engaging tasks
Build and lean on a social support network, as isolation tends to worsen catastrophizing and pain-related suffering

helplessness.label: Low
helplessness.description: Your helplessness score is low, indicating that you generally feel capable of coping with pain and do not perceive it as an overwhelming or uncontrollable experience. You maintain a sense of agency when dealing with pain.

helplessness.label: Moderate
helplessness.description: You show some feelings of helplessness regarding pain, occasionally feeling that you cannot go on or that pain will never improve. These thoughts are present but do not yet dominate your pain experience.

helplessness.label: High
helplessness.description: Your helplessness score is elevated, suggesting frequent feelings that pain is unbearable and beyond your ability to cope. Learning active coping strategies and challenging helpless thinking patterns can meaningfully reduce this distress.

helplessness.label: Very High
helplessness.description: Your helplessness is very high, indicating a pervasive sense that pain is overwhelming and uncontrollable. This level of helplessness strongly predicts poor pain outcomes and warrants professional psychological intervention.

magnification.label: Low
magnification.description: Your magnification score is low, suggesting that you do not tend to exaggerate the seriousness or threat of pain. You are able to maintain a realistic perspective on pain sensations without catastrophic amplification.

magnification.label: Moderate
magnification.description: You show some tendency to magnify the threat of pain, occasionally worrying that something serious may happen or that pain will worsen. This moderate amplification can increase anxiety around pain episodes.

magnification.label: High
magnification.description: Your magnification score is elevated, indicating a strong tendency to amplify pain sensations and expect the worst possible outcomes. This pattern heightens fear and anxiety, which in turn can increase actual pain perception.

magnification.label: Very High
magnification.description: Your magnification is very high, reflecting an intense tendency to perceive pain as extremely threatening and expect catastrophic consequences. This amplification cycle significantly worsens the overall pain experience.

rumination.label: Low
rumination.description: Your rumination score is low, indicating that you are generally able to redirect your attention away from pain rather than dwelling on it. You do not become mentally preoccupied with pain sensations or their implications.

rumination.label: Moderate
rumination.description: You show some tendency toward pain rumination, occasionally finding it difficult to stop thinking about pain or wanting it to go away. These repetitive thoughts are present but do not fully consume your attention.

rumination.label: High
rumination.description: Your rumination score is elevated, suggesting that you frequently cannot stop thinking about pain, how much it hurts, or how much you want it to stop. This mental preoccupation intensifies suffering beyond the physical sensation itself.

rumination.label: Very High
rumination.description: Your rumination is very high, indicating persistent and intrusive preoccupation with pain that dominates your mental life. This intense cognitive fixation on pain significantly amplifies distress and impairs your ability to function.
